story:
The bell over the door chimed. Marcell Crane didn't look up from the cuff he was stitching. He never rushed a seam—not for the living, not for the dead. The man who entered stood just inside the threshold, letting the quiet test him. Marcell counted three heartbeats before he spoke.

"Welcome to the Corroboree," he said.

"I need to schedule… a final fitting," the man replied, voice calm, jaw tight.

"Same measurements as last time?"

"No. This one's… bigger."

Marcell slid a black canvas suit bag across the counter. "Make sure the customer returns with this."

Two days later, the light in the shop was colder. Thursday had come. It was the fourth delivery that month, and the month wasn't done.

She was there again—Molly—perched on the arm of the leather chair like she owned it. Barefoot. Laddered nylons. A red slice across her throat that would never heal. Dead two years and refusing to leave, she wore her last cigarette like a promise—the one he'd given her.

"I thought I asked you not to sit there," he said.

"Oh, you can still see me." She gasped theatrically, then grinned. He made her put the cigarette out. It vanished before it hit the floor.

They moved through the shop's hush. A mannequin leered; a jacket bristled with pins; cufflinks gleamed like a spider's eyes. Molly's banter needled him.

"So, what happens when no one's left to be fitted? Do you start measuring yourself?"

"I don't plan on running out of work."

"Then who's gonna fit you one day?"

"They already tried," he said, and that made her smile, sharp as a pin.

She watched him the way drifting things watch anchors. For a moment the only sound was the soft hiss of the steam iron in the other room.

The tone sounded then—more felt than heard—from the bell in the back, where the real work was done. They froze. A door slammed somewhere beyond; an old suspension creaked; tires rolled away. Pachelbel, the blue hearse, had come and gone.

In the back room, a black canvas bag lay flat on the delivery table, left by whoever had carried it in. Marcell unzipped it. Two gold coins rested over the man's eyes, bright against waxy skin. Payment, plain and simple. He pocketed them. The dead didn't need change.

He drew a long breath. His eyes rolled back.

The world shifted. He stood in a stale hallway where glass crunched underfoot and picture frames lay shattered among peeling wallpaper. A television murmured ahead—laughter, crying, singing—a life in jump cuts. In the sitting room, a man faced the screen with a brandy in hand. Another glass waited.

"Come on, sit. You're gonna miss the best part," the man said, still not looking.

Marcell didn't drink with the dead. He lit a cigarette and offered one. Smoke leaked from a .45-caliber hole in the man's chin.

"Did you do that to yourself?" Marcell asked.

"No. But they wanted it to look like I did."

"Does it matter?" Marcell said gently. "I'm a professional. We're between, and not for long. I've come to facilitate your passing."

"And what does that look like?"

"Your patron booked the standard fitting. We'll have to go off the rack. Allow me."

He crossed to a paneled wall. Doors etched themselves into the wood, each with a Roman numeral. He opened VIII. Light poured out. When it faded, a final suit remained, waiting.

"Try it all on," Marcell said.

Back in the shop, he buttoned the jacket, smoothed the lapels, and closed the man's eyes. He lifted the body from the bag onto the steel rail and sent it sliding into the wide-bore chute. Far below, the tube opened to the sea. He listened until the hum faded. He rinsed the empty canvas bag and hung it to dry.

"One day, someone's gonna pay with counterfeit," Molly said from the doorway.

"Then I'll send them back," Marcell replied, reaching for his needle.

The front bell chimed again, and the ritual began anew.
